keyword: AML compliance system commercial banks Nigeria 2026
title: AML Compliance for Nigerian Commercial Banks — CBN 2026 Mandate | KORI
metaDescription: CBN Circular BSD/DIR/PUB/LAB/019/002 mandates automated AML for DMBs within 18 months. Implementation roadmap due June 10, 2026. See how KORI meets every CBN requirement.
h1: AML Compliance System for Nigerian Commercial Banks: The CBN 2026 Mandate Explained
urgencyStatement: 18-month deployment deadline for DMBs — roadmap due June 10, 2026.
problemStatement: Nigeria's 28 licensed commercial banks collectively process millions of daily transactions across NIBSS, mobile, POS, and correspondent banking rails. The CBN's March 10, 2026 circular (BSD/DIR/PUB/LAB/019/002) now mandates that every Deposit Money Bank operate a real-time, AI-powered automated AML system — not a rule-based filter, not a manual review queue. The institutions that misread this directive will face CBN sanctions, personal liability for compliance officers, and the operational cost of a forced system overhaul under regulatory scrutiny. BusinessDay Nigeria reported this week that Nigerian banks 'are not ready' — the institutions that move fastest gain a durable compliance advantage over those that wait.
cbnRequirements: Real-time transaction monitoring across all channels — mobile, NIBSS, POS, web, USSD — with automated alert generation (§4.1 of Baseline Standards)
Unified customer risk profiles that link KYC data to transaction behaviour, updated dynamically after every transaction (§4.2)
Real-time sanctions screening against OFAC, UN, and CBN watchlists on every transaction — not batch screening (§4.4)
Suspicious Transaction Report generation and NFIU submission within 24 hours of detection (§5.1.3)
Immutable, tamper-proof audit trail with role-based access controls, retained for minimum 5 years (§5.1.6)
Automated effectiveness reporting: STR conversion rate, false positive rate, average investigation time — available for CBN examination (§5.1.4)
AI and machine learning for anomaly detection, behavioural analytics, and risk scoring beyond threshold-based rules (§4.3)
Implementation roadmap submitted to CBN Compliance Department by June 10, 2026 (March 2026 Circular)
whyRulesBasedFails: Rule-based thresholds generate 60-80% false positive rates, overwhelming compliance teams and consuming analyst capacity on legitimate transactions
Rules review transactions independently and cannot detect connected fraud rings — accounts sharing the same device, IP, or phone number evade all individual transaction rules
Batch-processed rules create a 24-48 hour detection lag; CBN §4.1 now requires real-time detection
Static rules cannot adapt to evolving typologies; fraud rings change patterns within days of a new rule being deployed, while rule updates take weeks
Rules generate per-transaction alerts but cannot detect cross-account velocity patterns, network-level anomalies, or graph-based fraud structures
graphAdvantages: Graph-based entity mapping connects every transaction to its full network: user ↔ device ↔ IP ↔ phone ↔ wallet ↔ merchant — fraud rings become visible as clusters in real time
Machine learning risk scoring on 40+ behavioural signals reduces false positive rates below CBN's 30% target (§5.1.4), freeing compliance analysts for real investigations
Real-time processing across NIBSS, mobile, POS, web, and USSD simultaneously — no blind spots, no detection lag
Automated STR generation with NFIU-format export: timestamped, complete, and audit-ready within seconds of detection, consistently meeting the 24-hour filing requirement
CBN §5.1-aligned reporting dashboard with one-click export of STR conversion rate, false positive rate, and average investigation time for management and CBN examination
implementationSteps: Gap assessment (Week 1-2): Map your current AML capabilities against all 12 CBN baseline standard areas. KORI provides a free gap assessment tool at kori.seamount.io/compliance-audit that generates a CBN-ready report

Vendor evaluation and roadmap drafting (Weeks 2-6): Evaluate automated AML solutions against CBN requirements. Draft your implementation roadmap for CBN Compliance Department submission by June 10, 2026
API integration with core banking (Month 2): Connect KORI to your core banking system via webhook or direct API — KORI supports FLEXCUBE, Temenos T24, Finacle, and custom Nigerian bank systems

Data migration and customer profile enrichment (Months 2-3): Import historical transaction data to build initial customer risk profiles. Connect NIN/BVN validation via NIBSS API for ongoing identity enrichment
Testing and calibration (Month 3-4): Run KORI against historical transaction data. Calibrate risk thresholds to achieve CBN's <30% false positive target for your specific transaction mix
Go-live and continuous compliance monitoring (Month 4+): Deploy to production. KORI's dashboard provides real-time CBN compliance metrics for management reporting and regulator examination readiness

faqs.question: What does CBN Circular BSD/DIR/PUB/LAB/019/002 require for commercial banks?
faqs.answer: Circular BSD/DIR/PUB/LAB/019/002, issued March 10, 2026, requires all Deposit Money Banks to deploy automated AML systems covering real-time transaction monitoring, automated risk scoring, sanctions screening, and STR generation. DMBs have 18 months from March 10, 2026 for full compliance, but must submit a detailed implementation roadmap to the CBN Compliance Department by June 10, 2026 — 90 days from the circular date.

faqs.question: What happens if a Nigerian bank fails to submit the June 10 roadmap?
faqs.answer: The CBN circular explicitly states that non-compliance may result in remedial directives, administrative sanctions, and financial penalties against both the institution and accountable individuals — meaning compliance officers and senior management carry personal regulatory exposure, not just the organisation. The CBN monitors compliance through off-site surveillance, on-site examinations, and thematic reviews. A missed roadmap deadline is visible to the regulator and carries significant risk.

faqs.question: Can rule-based transaction monitoring satisfy the CBN AML mandate?
faqs.answer: No. The CBN baseline standards explicitly reference artificial intelligence and machine learning as required capabilities for anomaly detection. The standards also mandate real-time detection, unified customer risk profiles, and dynamic rule engines — none of which are achievable with static rule-based systems alone. The CBN requires systems that adapt to evolving typologies and detect patterns across the full customer relationship, not just individual transactions.

faqs.question: How long does it take to implement a compliant AML system for a Nigerian DMB?
faqs.answer: Implementation timelines vary by institution complexity and data quality. KORI's API-first architecture integrates with existing Nigerian core banking systems in weeks, not months. A basic compliant deployment for a mid-sized Nigerian bank typically takes 8-12 weeks from contract to go-live. The CBN's 18-month deadline provides sufficient runway, but the 90-day roadmap submission creates an immediate action requirement that should begin now.

faqs.question: What AML system features does the CBN specifically require under the 2026 standards?
faqs.answer: The CBN requires: real-time transaction monitoring across all channels, automated customer risk profiling, sanctions and PEP screening, STR generation and filing within 24 hours, fraud monitoring using AI/ML, a unified customer view integrating KYC with transaction monitoring, tamper-proof audit trails, role-based access controls, regulatory reporting dashboards, and documented vendor management policies for third-party AML providers.

faqs.question: What is the CBN false positive rate target and why does it matter?
faqs.answer: The CBN Baseline Standards (§5.1.4) set a target false positive rate below 30% — meaning fewer than 30% of AML alerts should be legitimate transactions incorrectly flagged. High false positive rates (above 50%) overwhelm compliance teams, indicate a miscalibrated system, and create regulatory exposure. KORI's graph-based risk scoring typically achieves false positive rates below 20% within 90 days of go-live for Nigerian DMBs.

faqs.question: How does KORI handle the NIN and BVN integration requirement?
faqs.answer: KORI integrates with NIBSS and NIMC APIs for real-time NIN and BVN validation during customer onboarding and ongoing due diligence. This supports the CBN requirement for KYC/KYB data to be integrated directly into transaction monitoring, providing the 'unified customer view' mandated by §4.2 of the baseline standards.

faqs.question: Which Nigerian banks are most urgently affected by the CBN AML mandate?
faqs.answer: All 28 licensed commercial banks face the 18-month deployment deadline. However, institutions named in the CBN's VASP AML pilot (launched April 2, 2026) face immediate supervisory scrutiny. Additionally, banks that received CBN sanctions in 2024-2025 for AML/KYC failures are operating under elevated regulatory attention and have the most urgent need to demonstrate compliant automated infrastructure.

title: AML Compliance for Nigerian Mobile Money Operators — CBN Mandate | KORI
metaDescription: 17 licensed Nigerian MMOs must deploy automated AML under CBN Circular BSD/DIR/PUB/LAB/019/002. Agent network fraud, SIM swap detection, USSD monitoring. Free audit.
h1: AML Compliance for Nigerian Mobile Money Operators: Agent Fraud, SIM Swap, and the CBN 2026 Mandate
urgencyStatement: 24-month deadline for MMOs — roadmap submission due June 10, 2026.
problemStatement: Nigeria's 17 licensed Mobile Money Operators process tens of millions of transactions monthly through agent networks, USSD, and mobile apps. The CBN's March 2026 baseline standards create a compliance challenge that is uniquely difficult for MMOs: agent network fraud, SIM swap attacks, and USSD channel abuse are Nigerian-specific fraud typologies that standard rule-based AML systems — built for Western banking environments — cannot detect. The June 10, 2026 roadmap deadline requires every MMO to demonstrate a credible plan to the CBN Compliance Department within 90 days of the March circular, making immediate action essential.
cbnRequirements: Real-time monitoring of all agent network transactions — including agent collusion pattern detection and shared device fingerprinting across agent networks (§4.1)
SIM swap event integration: escalate post-swap transactions above configurable thresholds within minutes of a SIM change notification from telcos (§4.2)
USSD transaction monitoring with velocity controls and cross-channel correlation with mobile and agent channels (§4.1)
Automated STR submission to NFIU within 24 hours for agent fraud, SIM-based fraud, and suspicious cross-border mobile money flows (§5.1.3)
Unified customer risk profile linking wallet address, phone number, NIN, BVN, and agent transaction history (§4.2)
Immutable audit trail for all agent transactions including geolocation data, device fingerprints, and agent identifiers (§5.1.6)
whyRulesBasedFails: Agent network fraud involves multiple agents colluding across geography — rule-based systems reviewing individual transactions cannot see the network-level pattern connecting them
SIM swap fraud happens in real time; batch-processed rules detect the fraud 24-48 hours after the funds have already been moved
USSD transactions lack device fingerprint data — rules cannot distinguish legitimate USSD from attacker-initiated USSD on a compromised SIM
High-volume agent networks generate thousands of legitimate large-value daily transactions; threshold-based rules create extreme false positive rates that are operationally unsustainable
Cross-channel correlation — the same attacker using agent, mobile, and USSD in sequence — requires connecting three data streams that rule-based systems treat as separate
graphAdvantages: Graph entity mapping links every agent, customer, device, phone, and location — agent collusion rings become visible as graph clusters before money leaves the network
SIM swap integration: KORI connects to Nigerian telco SIM swap event feeds (MTN, Airtel, Glo, 9mobile) and escalates all post-swap transactions with configurable risk scores
Cross-channel transaction graph: KORI ingests USSD, mobile, and agent transactions into a single entity graph, detecting cross-channel velocity attacks and structuring
Agent risk scoring: each of your agent network members receives a dynamic risk score based on transaction patterns, customer overlap, geographic clustering, and device sharing
Automated STR generation with NFIU-format export and typology codes specific to agent network fraud and SIM swap fraud
implementationSteps: Agent network data audit (Week 1-2): Inventory all active agents, agent devices, and agent transaction history. Identify existing fraud patterns in your historical data using KORI's analytics
KORI API integration with MMO platform (Weeks 2-4): Connect KORI's ingest API to your mobile money platform's transaction stream. KORI processes every transaction in real time regardless of channel
SIM swap feed integration (Month 2): Connect to telco SIM swap event APIs — KORI handles the technical integration and automatically escalates post-swap transactions
Agent risk profile setup (Month 2-3): Import all agent profiles and historical transaction data. KORI builds initial agent risk scores from historical behaviour in 48-72 hours
Threshold calibration against your transaction mix (Month 3): Run KORI against 6 months of historical agent transactions. Calibrate risk thresholds to hit CBN's <30% false positive target
Go-live and CBN reporting (Month 4+): Deploy to production. Generate your first CBN effectiveness report (STR rate, false positive rate, investigation time) within 30 days of go-live

faqs.question: What specific AML requirements apply to Nigerian MMOs under the CBN 2026 standards?
faqs.answer: CBN Circular BSD/DIR/PUB/LAB/019/002 applies to Mobile Money Operators with 24 months to full compliance from March 10, 2026. Key requirements include: real-time transaction monitoring across all channels (mobile, USSD, agent), automated STR submission to NFIU within 24 hours, unified customer risk profiles linked to NIN/BVN, agent network monitoring with geolocation, and an implementation roadmap submitted to the CBN Compliance Department by June 10, 2026.

faqs.question: How should Nigerian MMOs detect SIM swap fraud under the CBN 2026 standards?
faqs.answer: The CBN baseline standards require dynamic risk profiling incorporating behavioural signals. For SIM swap fraud, this means integrating SIM swap event data from Nigerian telcos directly into the AML transaction monitoring system. Transactions initiated within a configurable window after a SIM swap event — typically 24-72 hours — should automatically receive elevated risk scores and trigger enhanced due diligence or transaction blocking.

faqs.question: What is agent network fraud and how does automated AML detect it?
faqs.answer: Agent network fraud involves registered mobile money agents colluding to process fraudulent transactions — structuring cash deposits below reporting thresholds, creating fake customer accounts, or splitting large transactions across multiple agents in coordinated patterns. Automated AML detects this through graph-based analysis: when multiple agents share devices, customers, or transaction patterns that individually appear normal, the graph engine identifies the connected cluster as anomalous and triggers an alert.

faqs.question: What is the cost of a CBN-compliant AML system for a Nigerian MMO?
faqs.answer: Enterprise global AML platforms like NICE Actimize start at $500,000+ annually and require 12-18 months of implementation. These are not realistic options for most Nigerian MMOs. KORI is built specifically for Nigerian-market institutions with API-based pricing that scales with transaction volume — starting from ₦500,000/month for MMO tier. Given the cost of a CBN fine (which reached ₦1 billion for major fintechs in 2025), the compliance investment has a clear ROI.

faqs.question: Can KORI integrate with Nigerian MMO core platforms?
faqs.answer: Yes. KORI uses a webhook-based API architecture that integrates with any transaction platform that can emit events — including Interswitch, NIBSS-connected platforms, and custom MMO backends. The integration typically takes 1-2 weeks of technical work. KORI also supports batch file ingestion for MMOs without real-time API capability as an initial integration path.

faqs.question: What happens to MMOs that miss the June 10, 2026 CBN roadmap deadline?
faqs.answer: The CBN circular explicitly states sanctions may affect both the institution and accountable individuals. Missing the roadmap deadline — which is tracked by the CBN Compliance Department — signals to regulators that the institution has not begun addressing the mandate. This increases the likelihood of a targeted CBN examination and reduces goodwill with regulators during the implementation period.

faqs.question: Does KORI support USSD transaction monitoring for Nigerian MMOs?
faqs.answer: Yes. KORI ingests USSD transaction events alongside mobile app and agent network transactions into a single entity graph. This cross-channel correlation is essential for detecting USSD-based attacks where the same bad actor uses multiple channels in sequence to test credentials or structure transactions below individual channel thresholds.

faqs.question: What reporting does KORI generate for MMOs under CBN §5.1.4?
faqs.answer: KORI generates all reporting required by CBN §5.1.4 for MMOs: STR conversion rate, false positive rate, average investigation time, alert volume by risk band, and agent network risk summary. Reports are exportable as CSV and structured for direct use in CBN examination submissions. Every metric references the specific CBN baseline standard section, giving compliance officers audit-ready documentation.

keyword: VASP Travel Rule compliance Nigeria FATF Recommendation 16
title: VASP Travel Rule Compliance Nigeria — FATF R.16 & CBN Pilot Guide | KORI
metaDescription: Nigerian VASPs in the CBN AML pilot must comply with FATF Travel Rule (Recommendation 16). Real-time crypto monitoring, Travel Rule data exchange, STR automation.
h1: Travel Rule Compliance for Nigerian VASPs: CBN Pilot Requirements and FATF Recommendation 16
cbnSection: FATF R.15, R.16 and CBN VASP Guidelines 2023
urgencyStatement: VASPs in the CBN pilot face immediate supervisory scrutiny — not a future deadline.
problemStatement: Nigeria processed $92.1 billion in cryptocurrency transactions between July 2024 and June 2025 — nearly three times South Africa's volume and by far the largest crypto market in Africa (Chainalysis, 2025). The CBN's VASP AML supervision pilot (launched April 2, 2026) places major Nigerian platforms including Flutterwave, Paystack, Yellow Card, Quidax, and Busha under direct regulatory examination. For these VASPs, compliance is not a 24-month future exercise — it is happening now. The Travel Rule (FATF Recommendation 16) requires that counterparty VASP data be exchanged on every transaction above threshold, a capability most Nigerian VASPs have not yet implemented. Failing the pilot is not just a fine — it is a permanent reputation problem in a market where regulatory trust is the primary competitive differentiator.
cbnRequirements: FATF Travel Rule compliance: collect, verify, and share originator and beneficiary data with counterparty VASPs on transactions above USD 1,000 threshold (FATF R.16)
Blockchain analytics integration for on-chain transaction risk scoring, wallet clustering, and source-of-funds verification for self-hosted wallets
Real-time STR generation for suspicious crypto transactions with NFIU submission within 24 hours — including crypto-specific typology codes
Enhanced due diligence for all interactions with self-hosted wallets at on-ramp and off-ramp points
Real-time transaction monitoring covering all supported assets: BTC, ETH, USDT-TRC20, USDT-ERC20, cNGN stablecoin, and exchange-traded tokens
Automated sanctions screening of wallet addresses against OFAC SDN list, UN consolidated list, and CBN watchlists on every transaction
whyRulesBasedFails: Blockchain transactions have no geographic equivalent of a bank branch — rule-based geographic triggers do not work for wallet-to-wallet transfers across chains
Crypto fraud rings use chain-hopping, mixing services, and cross-chain bridges to obscure fund flows — simple threshold rules cannot track these multi-hop patterns
Travel Rule compliance requires real-time counterparty VASP identification and structured data exchange — rule-based systems have no mechanism for this
On-chain velocity attacks happen in seconds across multiple wallets — by the time a batch rule detects them, the funds have already cleared multiple hops
Wallet clustering (identifying that 50 wallets are controlled by the same entity) requires graph machine learning — simple rules cannot perform entity resolution across blockchain addresses
graphAdvantages: On-chain graph analysis: KORI maps blockchain transaction flows, clusters related wallets using graph ML, and assigns risk scores to addresses based on their transaction history and network connections
Travel Rule engine: KORI automates counterparty VASP identification and Travel Rule data exchange using the IVMS101 messaging standard — compliant with FATF R.16 out of the box
Cross-chain monitoring: KORI tracks fund flows across Bitcoin, Ethereum, Tron (USDT-TRC20), Solana, and cNGN in a single entity graph, detecting cross-chain structuring
Self-hosted wallet enhanced due diligence: KORI automatically escalates transactions involving self-hosted wallets for EDD workflows, with configurable source-of-funds questionnaires
On-chain sanctions screening: every incoming and outgoing wallet address is screened against OFAC, UN, and CBN watchlists in real time before transaction processing
implementationSteps: Travel Rule gap assessment (Week 1): Identify which transaction corridors require Travel Rule data exchange. Map your current counterparty VASP relationships and assess existing data collection capabilities
Blockchain analytics integration (Weeks 2-4): Connect KORI's blockchain analytics module to your exchange or wallet infrastructure. KORI supports Chainalysis, Elliptic, and direct on-chain data feeds
Travel Rule messaging setup (Month 2): Implement the IVMS101 messaging protocol for counterparty VASP data exchange. KORI handles message formatting, encryption, delivery, and receipt confirmation
Wallet sanctions screening automation (Month 2-3): Configure real-time OFAC/UN/CBN wallet address screening for all deposits and withdrawals — automated before transaction execution
CBN pilot compliance reporting (Month 3): Generate the effectiveness report required by the CBN pilot programme, demonstrating Travel Rule compliance rates, detection metrics, and STR filing history
Ongoing monitoring and pilot examination preparation (Month 4+): KORI provides a CBN pilot-ready compliance package including all documentation required for on-site examination

faqs.question: What is the FATF Travel Rule and why must Nigerian VASPs comply?
faqs.answer: FATF Recommendation 16 (the Travel Rule) requires Virtual Asset Service Providers to collect, verify, and share originator and beneficiary information for crypto transfers above USD 1,000 or equivalent. Nigeria's removal from the FATF Grey List in October 2025 was contingent on demonstrating Travel Rule implementation. The CBN VASP pilot (April 2026) specifically assesses Travel Rule compliance among major Nigerian platforms.

faqs.question: Which Nigerian VASPs are in the CBN AML supervision pilot?
faqs.answer: The CBN pilot (launched April 2, 2026) includes several prominent Nigerian fintechs and crypto platforms. Publicly named institutions include Flutterwave and Paystack, with Yellow Card, Quidax, and Busha also reported as pilot participants. The pilot is regulatory supervision — not optional. VASPs in the cohort face heightened scrutiny and must demonstrate compliant AML infrastructure on examination.

faqs.question: What does Travel Rule compliance require technically for a Nigerian VASP?
faqs.answer: Travel Rule compliance requires: collecting originator name, wallet address, and identification data before processing outgoing transfers above threshold; identifying the receiving VASP and transmitting originator data before or simultaneously with the transfer; collecting and verifying beneficiary data on incoming transfers; and maintaining records for 5 years. This requires integration with a Travel Rule messaging protocol (IVMS101) and a counterparty VASP directory.

faqs.question: What is Nigeria's cNGN stablecoin and does it require Travel Rule compliance?
faqs.answer: The cNGN is a naira-pegged stablecoin launched in February 2025 by the Africa Stablecoin Consortium, available on exchanges including Quidax and Busha. As a virtual asset under the CBN VASP Guidelines 2023 and the Investments and Securities Act 2025, cNGN transactions fall under Travel Rule obligations above threshold. Platforms supporting cNGN must monitor cNGN transfers for Travel Rule compliance exactly as they would monitor USDT or Bitcoin transfers.

faqs.question: What happens if a VASP in the CBN pilot fails the Travel Rule compliance assessment?
faqs.answer: VASPs failing the CBN pilot compliance assessment face regulatory enforcement under the CBN Act and AML Act 2022. This can include suspension of licensed operations, mandatory system upgrades under CBN supervision, financial penalties, and — in cases of significant failure — licence revocation. The CBN demonstrated willingness to act in 2025 when two major fintechs were collectively fined ₦1 billion for AML/KYC failures.

faqs.question: How does KORI's blockchain analytics differ from standard AML transaction monitoring?
faqs.answer: Standard AML monitors fiat transactions in bank databases. KORI's blockchain analytics module monitors on-chain transactions directly — tracing funds across wallet addresses, identifying clustering patterns that indicate shared control, and flagging interactions with high-risk wallets (mixers, darknet markets, sanctioned entities). This on-chain view is combined with the off-chain customer profile in KORI's graph, giving compliance officers a complete picture of crypto fund flows across both on-chain and off-chain activity.

faqs.question: How long does Travel Rule implementation take for a Nigerian VASP?
faqs.answer: A full Travel Rule implementation — including counterparty VASP directory, IVMS101 messaging, and blockchain analytics — typically takes 8-12 weeks for a platform with existing API infrastructure. KORI's VASP module is pre-configured for the Nigerian regulatory context and can be deployed faster. Given the CBN pilot's immediate supervisory scrutiny, we recommend starting implementation now with a target completion within 60 days.

faqs.question: Does KORI integrate with Nigerian crypto exchanges directly?
faqs.answer: Yes. KORI integrates with Nigerian exchange APIs including Quidax, Busha, Yellow Card, and Flutterwave for real-time transaction event monitoring. KORI also supports direct blockchain node connections for on-chain monitoring independent of exchange APIs. For VASPs with existing Chainalysis or Elliptic integrations, KORI can consume their risk scoring data and integrate it into the entity graph.

title: AML Compliance for Nigerian Microfinance Banks — CBN 2026 Mandate | KORI

urgencyStatement: 745 Nigerian MFBs face the same mandate. Roadmap due June 10, 2026.

Most MFBs operate with lean compliance teams, often one or two people managing compliance alongside other responsibilities. Manual AML processes that might be operationally sustainable today become compliance violations the moment the CBN's June 10, 2026 roadmap deadline passes. The baseline standards do not exempt MFBs based on institution size — proportionate implementation is required, not zero implementation. Every MFB needs a credible, costed roadmap in the CBN Compliance Department's inbox by June 10.

whyRulesBasedFails: Manual STR filing takes days for small compliance teams; the 24-hour NFIU submission requirement is impossible to meet consistently without automation

Manual sanctions screening is too slow and labour-intensive for MFBs with thousands of customers and daily growing transaction volumes

faqs.question: Are Nigerian microfinance banks required to comply with the CBN AML automation mandate?
faqs.answer: Yes. CBN Circular BSD/DIR/PUB/LAB/019/002 explicitly includes all CBN-regulated financial institutions, covering all 745 licensed Microfinance Banks. The circular requires 'Other Financial Institutions' — which includes MFBs — to deploy automated AML within 24 months from March 10, 2026. All institutions regardless of size must submit a detailed implementation roadmap to the CBN Compliance Department by June 10, 2026.

faqs.question: How can a small Nigerian MFB afford automated AML software?
faqs.answer: Traditional enterprise AML platforms (NICE Actimize, Temenos AML) cost $200,000-$500,000+ annually with 12-18 month implementations — not viable for most Nigerian MFBs. KORI is designed specifically for the Nigerian market with API-based pricing that scales with transaction volume. For a smaller MFB, KORI's monthly cost is comparable to one additional compliance officer's salary, while providing coverage that a team of five could not match manually.

faqs.question: What fraud types are most common in Nigerian MFBs and how does automated AML detect them?
faqs.answer: The most common MFB fraud typologies in Nigeria include: group lending fraud (multiple borrowers colluding with shared contacts or addresses), insider fraud (staff approving loans to connected parties), cash deposit structuring (splitting deposits below ₦5 million CTR threshold), and identity fraud using stolen NIN/BVN credentials. KORI's graph engine is specifically calibrated to Nigerian MFB typologies, detecting network-level patterns that individual transaction rules miss entirely.

faqs.question: What is the minimum viable AML system for a Nigerian MFB to satisfy CBN requirements?
faqs.answer: The CBN baseline standards require proportionate implementation based on institution size, transaction volume, and risk profile. For a smaller MFB, the minimum viable compliant system includes: automated transaction monitoring with configurable thresholds, customer risk profiling linked to BVN/NIN, automated sanctions screening, STR generation capability with NFIU submission, and a tamper-proof audit trail. KORI's MFB tier covers all of these requirements.

faqs.question: How does KORI help MFBs with the June 10, 2026 CBN roadmap submission?
faqs.answer: KORI provides a compliance dashboard that maps your MFB's current AML capabilities against each of the CBN baseline standard requirements. The dashboard generates a gap analysis report and a pre-formatted implementation roadmap in the CBN submission format. MFBs that have completed the KORI free audit can use the generated report directly as the basis for their June 10 submission.

faqs.question: Does KORI support group lending fraud detection for Nigerian MFBs?
faqs.answer: Yes. Group lending fraud detection is one of KORI's core capabilities for the MFB market. KORI's graph engine systematically analyses all borrowers' connection data — shared phone numbers, addresses, contacts, and device fingerprints — across the entire loan portfolio. When multiple borrowers in a loan group share connected data that should be independent, KORI flags the group application for compliance review before loan disbursement.

faqs.question: How long does KORI take to implement for a typical Nigerian MFB?
faqs.answer: For a typical Nigerian MFB with a standard core banking system, KORI implementation takes 4-8 weeks from contract to go-live. This covers: API integration with the core banking system (1-2 weeks), historical customer data migration and BVN validation (1-2 weeks), threshold calibration against historical transactions (1-2 weeks), and compliance team training (1 week). The June 10, 2026 roadmap deadline is well within reach for MFBs that begin now.

faqs.question: What reporting does KORI generate for a Nigerian MFB's CBN examination?
faqs.answer: KORI generates all reporting required for CBN examination: alert log with detection timestamps and resolution outcomes, all STRs filed with NFIU confirmation receipts, case files showing the decision chain from alert to resolution, monthly STR conversion rates and false positive rates (§5.1.4), and the annual AML effectiveness report. All documentation references specific CBN baseline standard sections, giving examiners the citation trail they need.

keyword: STR filing requirements Nigeria NFIU 24 hours 2026
title: STR Filing Nigeria 2026 — NFIU 24-Hour Requirement Guide | KORI
metaDescription: CBN requires STR filing with NFIU within 24 hours under the 2026 baseline standards. Complete guide for Nigerian compliance officers: requirements, format, automation.
h1: Suspicious Transaction Report Filing in Nigeria 2026: Meeting the NFIU 24-Hour Deadline
urgencyStatement: 24-hour STR filing requirement is in effect now under CBN §5.1.3.
problemStatement: STR filing is the operational heart of AML compliance in Nigeria, and it is the area where most institutions fail under CBN examination. The CBN Baseline Standards (§5.1.3) require Suspicious Transaction Reports to be filed with the NFIU within 24 hours of detection. For institutions still running manual review workflows — where an analyst flags a transaction, a manager reviews it, a compliance officer approves the STR, and someone formats and submits the report — this 24-hour window is regularly missed. BusinessDay Nigeria reported this month that non-compliant institutions face not just fines but personal liability for compliance officers. Automated STR generation is the only reliable path to consistent 24-hour compliance.
cbnRequirements: STR must be filed with NFIU within 24 hours of detecting a suspicious transaction — this 24-hour clock starts at detection, not at case approval (§5.1.3)
STR must include: transaction details, customer identification (BVN/NIN), suspicion grounds with written narrative, account history, and identifying information for the analyst and approving officer
Tipping off prohibition: customers must not be informed that an STR has been or may be filed — violations carry criminal liability (AML Act 2022)
Immutable record of all STRs filed retained for minimum 5 years and accessible during CBN on-site examination (§5.1.6)
Monthly STR volume and conversion rate reporting available for CBN examination (§5.1.4)
Currency Transaction Reports (CTRs) for cash transactions above ₦5 million (individuals) or ₦10 million (corporates) — filed separately from STRs via NFIU goAML
whyRulesBasedFails: Manual STR drafting takes 4-8 hours per report for a trained analyst — institutions processing even moderate alert volumes routinely miss the 24-hour NFIU window
Inconsistent report quality: manually drafted STRs vary significantly in narrative completeness, creating examination risk when CBN reviewers assess the adequacy of reporting
No automated escalation chain: the handoff from analyst to manager to compliance officer is tracked in emails and spreadsheets — with no audit trail and no SLA enforcement
Duplicate reporting: without a central case management system, the same suspicious pattern can generate multiple STRs filed by different teams — a compliance and intelligence quality failure
Tipping-off risk: compliance officers manually gathering information for an STR may inadvertently contact customers in ways that constitute tipping off — a criminal violation under the AML Act 2022
graphAdvantages: Automated STR drafting: KORI generates a complete NFIU goAML-format STR narrative from the alert data — the analyst reviews and approves in 15 minutes instead of writing from scratch in 4 hours
24-hour SLA tracking: KORI tracks time from alert detection to STR submission, sends automated escalation alerts when the 24-hour window is at risk, and records all timestamps in the audit trail
Case management with approval workflow: suspicious alerts promote to cases, cases link to STRs, all with a complete digital audit trail from detection through NFIU confirmation
Duplicate detection: KORI links related alerts to the same case, preventing duplicate STR filing on connected suspicious activity across accounts or channels
Tipping-off risk controls: KORI flags case management actions that carry tipping-off risk and provides guidance on permissible investigation steps at each stage
implementationSteps: Current STR workflow audit (Week 1): Map your institution's current detection-to-submission workflow. Measure actual time-to-file across the last 30 days of STRs — most institutions are surprised by the gap
Case management configuration (Weeks 2-3): Configure KORI's case management module with your institution's escalation hierarchy, approval levels, and SLA thresholds

NFIU goAML format configuration (Weeks 3-4): Configure KORI's STR template to match NFIU submission requirements for your institution type — KORI maintains updated templates as NFIU updates its format

Automated alert-to-case escalation (Month 2): Connect KORI's alert engine to case management. High-risk alerts automatically create cases, notify the responsible analyst, and start the 24-hour SLA clock
Compliance team training (Month 2-3): Train your compliance team on the case management workflow. Run test scenarios with synthetic suspicious transactions to validate the end-to-end process
Go-live and SLA performance monitoring (Month 3+): Deploy KORI to production. Track 24-hour STR SLA compliance as a management KPI with automated weekly reports

faqs.question: What is the NFIU and how are STRs submitted in Nigeria?
faqs.answer: The Nigerian Financial Intelligence Unit (NFIU) is the government agency that receives, analyzes, and disseminates financial intelligence. STRs are submitted electronically through the NFIU's goAML system, to which all CBN-regulated institutions must be registered. KORI generates STRs in goAML-compatible XML format, supports direct submission, and maintains submission confirmation records in the audit trail.

faqs.question: What is the difference between an STR and a CTR in Nigeria?
faqs.answer: A Suspicious Transaction Report (STR) is filed when any transaction is suspected to involve money laundering, terrorist financing, or fraud — regardless of amount. A Currency Transaction Report (CTR) is filed for cash transactions above ₦5 million (individuals) or ₦10 million (corporate customers), regardless of whether the transaction is suspicious. Both are filed with the NFIU via goAML but as separate report types. KORI generates and tracks both STRs and CTRs.

faqs.question: What personal liability do Nigerian compliance officers face for late STR filing?
faqs.answer: CBN Circular BSD/DIR/PUB/LAB/019/002 explicitly states sanctions may affect 'accountable individuals' — not just the institution. Under the AML Act 2022, individuals responsible for AML compliance can face personal financial penalties and criminal liability for wilful failure to report suspicious transactions. This personal exposure is increasingly real: the CBN's ₦1 billion fine on two major fintechs in 2025 signalled that enforcement extends beyond institutional accountability.

faqs.question: What constitutes tipping off in the context of Nigerian STR filing?
faqs.answer: Tipping off — defined in the AML Act 2022 — is informing a customer or any connected party that an STR has been or may be filed. It is a criminal offence. The challenge for compliance teams is that legitimate information gathering (such as asking a customer to explain a transaction) can constitute tipping off if the enquiry reveals the institution's suspicion. KORI's case management workflow includes tipping-off risk flags and guidance on permissible investigation steps at each case stage.

faqs.question: What information must be included in a Nigerian STR for NFIU submission?
faqs.answer: An NFIU-compliant Nigerian STR must include: transaction date, amount, and type; originator account details including BVN and NIN where available; beneficiary details; a written narrative describing the suspicious behaviour and the grounds for suspicion; details of any prior STRs on the same customer; the analyst's name and the approving compliance officer; and the institution's NFIU reference number. KORI pre-populates all mandatory fields from transaction data and alert information, reducing STR drafting time to under 15 minutes.

faqs.question: What is the CBN's STR conversion rate target and how is it measured?
faqs.answer: The CBN Baseline Standards (§5.1.4) require institutions to track STR conversion rate — the proportion of AML alerts that result in STR filings. A well-calibrated system typically converts 5-15% of alerts to STRs. The CBN's target is a conversion rate above 5% — meaning at least 5% of alerts should be genuinely suspicious. A rate below 5% may indicate the AML system is generating too many false positive alerts, which is itself a compliance concern.

faqs.question: How does KORI handle institutions that miss the 24-hour STR window?
faqs.answer: KORI sends automated escalation alerts via email and dashboard notification when a case's STR is approaching the 24-hour window — at 16 hours and again at 22 hours. If the 24-hour window is missed, KORI records the breach in the audit trail with the reason and timestamps for the post-incident report. The audit trail enables compliance officers to demonstrate to CBN examiners the exact sequence of events — important context for cases where the delay was due to genuine investigative complexity rather than process failure.

faqs.question: What documentation does KORI provide for a CBN AML examination focused on STR compliance?
faqs.answer: For a CBN examination of STR compliance, KORI provides: a complete alert log with detection timestamps and resolution outcomes; all STRs filed with NFIU submission timestamps and confirmation receipts; case files showing the full decision chain from alert to STR approval; monthly STR volume and conversion rate reports (§5.1.4); and a summary of any 24-hour breaches with root cause documentation. All exports reference specific CBN §5.1 sections, giving examiners the citation trail they require.

keyword: graph-based fraud detection Nigeria vs rule-based systems
title: Graph-Based vs Rule-Based Fraud Detection Nigeria — Which Meets CBN Standards? | KORI
metaDescription: Why rule-based AML systems fail Nigeria's CBN 2026 mandate. Graph-based fraud ring detection catches what rules miss. Real fraud patterns from the Nigerian market.
h1: Graph-Based vs Rule-Based Fraud Detection in Nigeria: Why the CBN's 2026 Mandate Requires a Different Approach
urgencyStatement: CBN §4.3 explicitly requires AI/ML — rule-based systems alone do not comply.
problemStatement: The Nigerian financial industry has spent the last decade deploying rule-based transaction monitoring: if the amount exceeds a threshold, flag it; if a customer appears on a sanctions list, block them. These rules work for simple, isolated suspicious transactions. They do not work for the organised fraud that dominates Nigerian financial crime: coordinated account takeovers run by groups sharing a single device, 'credit alert' scams operating across networks of mule accounts, and agent banking fraud rings coordinating across dozens of registered agents. The CBN's 2026 baseline standards recognised this explicitly — §4.3 mandates the use of AI and machine learning for anomaly detection, going beyond what static rules can achieve. This guide explains why, and what graph-based detection actually catches.
cbnRequirements: AI and machine learning for anomaly detection beyond threshold-based rule systems — the standards explicitly cite these technologies as requirements (§4.3)
Behavioural analytics that detect patterns across the full customer relationship, not just individual transactions (§4.2)
Entity relationship mapping that connects customers, devices, IPs, wallets, and phones into a unified risk view (§4.2)
Dynamic risk scoring updated continuously with new transaction data, not recalculated in nightly batch runs (§4.2)
Detection of fraud ring structures — connected networks of accounts used in coordinated fraud — that are invisible to per-transaction rules (§4.3)
whyRulesBasedFails: Rules detect fraud within accounts; graph analysis detects fraud across accounts — the organised fraud that costs Nigerian institutions the most is cross-account by design
Rules are static; fraud rings change patterns in days once they learn a new rule has been deployed — graph ML learns continuously from new data without manual rule updates
Rules generate alerts per-transaction with no context; graph analysis generates alerts per-entity with full network context, reducing false positives by 60-80%
Rules require a human to design each scenario in advance; ML detects novel fraud patterns that no human has yet encountered and codified into a rule
Rules process each transaction independently; graph analysis processes every transaction as part of an evolving entity network — the connection IS the evidence
graphAdvantages: Entity graph detection: three accounts sharing one mobile device, one IP address, and one phone number — invisible to rules, instantly flagged as a potential fraud ring by graph analysis

Label propagation: when one account is confirmed as fraudulent, graph ML automatically propagates fraud risk to all connected accounts sharing devices, IPs, or contacts — catching the ring, not just the individual
Continuous learning: KORI's ML model updates risk scores continuously as new transactions arrive, adapting to evolving fraud patterns without waiting for a compliance officer to write a new rule
Nigerian typology library: KORI is pre-configured with fraud ring patterns specific to the Nigerian market — 'credit alert' mule networks, agent collusion clusters, SIM swap account takeover chains

Explainable risk scores: KORI shows compliance officers exactly why an account was flagged — 'shared device with 4 other flagged accounts' or 'IP address seen in 12 suspicious transactions this week' — supporting the audit trail required by CBN §5.1.6
implementationSteps: Baseline analysis: what are you currently detecting? (Week 1): Use KORI's free audit tool to assess your current detection coverage against known Nigerian fraud typologies. Identify blind spots in your existing rule-based system

Graph schema design for your institution (Weeks 2-3): Map the entity types relevant to your institution — accounts, devices, IPs, phones, agents, merchants — and the relationships KORI will track between them

Historical data ingestion and initial graph build (Month 1-2): Import 12+ months of historical transaction data. KORI builds the entity graph and identifies retrospective fraud patterns that existing rules missed

Rule-to-graph migration (Month 2-3): Work with KORI to migrate your existing rules into graph-aware scenarios. Existing thresholds become graph-contextual thresholds — the same amount flagged differently depending on the account's network
ML model calibration (Month 3): Calibrate KORI's ML model to your institution's specific transaction patterns. Label known fraud cases from your historical data to improve initial model accuracy
Go-live with parallel running (Month 4): Deploy KORI alongside your existing rule-based system. Compare detection coverage for 30 days before full migration

faqs.question: What is a fraud ring in the Nigerian banking context?
faqs.answer: A fraud ring is a coordinated group of accounts, people, or agents working together to commit financial fraud. In Nigeria, common fraud rings include: groups of mule accounts receiving and laundering proceeds from 'credit alert' SMS scams; networks of agent banking agents splitting large cash deposits to avoid CTR reporting thresholds; coordinated account takeover groups who share SIM cards and devices across victims; and organised business email compromise operations using multiple Nigerian bank accounts as landing accounts.

faqs.question: How does graph-based analysis detect fraud rings that rules miss?
faqs.answer: Graph analysis maps the relationships between every entity in the financial system — accounts, phones, devices, IPs, addresses, and agents. When five accounts that appear individually legitimate all share the same device fingerprint, IP address, or phone number, the graph identifies them as a connected cluster. Confirmation of fraud in one account automatically elevates risk scores for all connected accounts. Rules cannot perform this cross-account analysis because they are designed to evaluate one transaction or one account at a time.

faqs.question: What is label propagation in AML fraud detection?
faqs.answer: Label propagation is a graph machine learning technique where confirmed fraud labels spread through the entity network along connection edges. When a compliance officer confirms that Account A is fraudulent, label propagation automatically calculates increased fraud risk for Account B (which shares Account A's device), Account C (which shared an IP with Account B), and Account D (which received funds from Account C). This automated network risk propagation is one of the most powerful capabilities in KORI's graph engine.

faqs.question: Why does the CBN specifically require AI and machine learning in the 2026 baseline standards?
faqs.answer: The CBN baseline standards reference AI and ML in §4.3 because regulators recognised that the transaction volume and complexity of Nigeria's digital payments ecosystem has exceeded what static rules can reliably monitor. Nigeria's National Payment Stack processes transactions in milliseconds — there is no time for human review of individual transactions. AI and ML enable continuous, real-time, adaptive monitoring at scale. The CBN also noted that institutions relying solely on rules were producing inadequate STR quality and volumes relative to known fraud levels.

faqs.question: How does KORI's Nigerian typology library work?
faqs.answer: KORI is pre-configured with fraud pattern templates based on documented Nigerian financial crime typologies — including credit alert mule networks, agent collusion fraud, SIM swap account takeover chains, and round-tripping patterns common in NIBSS transactions. These templates define the graph structure and velocity patterns that indicate each fraud type. When KORI identifies a cluster matching a known typology, it labels the alert with the specific fraud type, making compliance officer triage significantly faster.

faqs.question: Is graph-based AML expensive to implement compared to rule-based systems?
faqs.answer: Graph-based AML was historically expensive — requiring dedicated graph database infrastructure, data science teams, and long implementation cycles. KORI delivers graph-based detection as a managed API service, eliminating the infrastructure and data science overhead. For Nigerian institutions, KORI is priced comparably to traditional rule-based SaaS compliance platforms — often less expensive when the total cost of compliance analyst hours saved is calculated.

faqs.question: How does KORI explain graph-based risk scores to compliance officers and CBN examiners?
faqs.answer: KORI's explainability module provides a plain-language risk narrative for every alert: 'This account is flagged because it shares a device fingerprint with 3 accounts that have confirmed fraud history, and the transaction velocity increased 400% in the last 24 hours following a SIM swap event on the registered phone number.' This narrative is attached to the case file and included in the STR. For CBN examiners, KORI can produce a visual graph of any flagged entity's network, showing every connection that contributed to the risk score.

faqs.question: Can KORI work alongside an existing rule-based system?
faqs.answer: Yes — and for most Nigerian institutions, a parallel run period is advisable. KORI can ingest the same transaction stream as an existing rule-based system, running graph-based analysis in parallel. After 30-60 days, compliance teams can compare detection coverage, false positive rates, and alert quality between the two approaches before migrating fully to KORI. This removes migration risk and provides empirical evidence for the compliance team and management of graph-based detection's superiority for Nigerian fraud typologies.

keyword: CBN AML compliance readiness score tool Nigeria free
title: Free CBN AML Compliance Readiness Score — Know Your Gap Before June 10 | KORI
metaDescription: Free 12-question CBN AML compliance audit. Get your institution's readiness score mapped to CBN Baseline Standards §4-6. PDF report. 5 minutes. No credit card.
h1: Free CBN AML Compliance Readiness Score: Know Where You Stand Before the June 10 Deadline
urgencyStatement: June 10, 2026 roadmap deadline is in 62 days. Know your gap now.
problemStatement: Every compliance officer at every CBN-regulated institution is currently asking the same question: are we compliant enough to submit a credible roadmap by June 10? Most institutions do not have an honest, structured answer. They know they have gaps — but they do not know which gaps are critical, which are medium-priority, and which can wait for the 18-24 month deployment period. The CBN baseline standards cover 12 distinct technical areas, from real-time transaction monitoring to audit trail requirements to false positive rate targets. Without a systematic assessment, compliance officers are making CBN roadmap submissions based on incomplete information — which creates regulatory risk if the CBN examines the roadmap against the actual institutional baseline.
cbnRequirements: Assessment of all 12 CBN baseline standard areas before submitting the implementation roadmap (March 2026 Circular)
Honest gap analysis identifying current capabilities versus required capabilities for each baseline standard area
Credible implementation plan with realistic timelines for closing identified gaps, submitted by June 10, 2026
Evidence that the institution has a structured approach to AML compliance — not just a list of technology purchases
whyRulesBasedFails: Self-assessment without a structured framework misses gaps — compliance officers tend to rate their systems more generously than CBN examiners do
Generic global AML framework assessments do not map to the specific CBN baseline standards, leaving Nigerian-specific requirements unaddressed
Consultant-led gap assessments cost ₦2-5 million and take 4-6 weeks — too slow and expensive for the 90-day roadmap window
Internal assessments lack the regulatory reference depth to generate CBN-ready documentation
graphAdvantages: 12-question assessment covering every CBN baseline standard area — completed in 5 minutes with automated scoring
Results mapped to specific CBN section references (§4.1 through §6.2) — the exact citations compliance officers need for their roadmap submissions
PDF report downloadable immediately — use it directly as the gap analysis section of your CBN June 10 roadmap submission
Personalised KORI demo pre-configured to your specific gaps — compliance officers see exactly how KORI closes their institution's specific weaknesses
Completely free — no credit card, no commitment, no sales call required to complete the assessment
implementationSteps: Take the audit (5 minutes): Complete all 12 questions at kori.seamount.io/compliance-audit — answer based on your institution's current state, not your aspirations
Review your score and gap analysis: Your score maps to CBN baseline standards. Critical gaps (scored 0 on a CBN-required area) require immediate attention in your roadmap
Download your PDF report: The PDF includes your score breakdown, gap analysis by CBN section, and recommended remediation steps — suitable for direct use in your CBN roadmap submission
Share with your leadership team: Use the gap analysis to brief your board, CEO, and CTO on the compliance investment required. The CBN citation trail helps make the business case

Book a KORI demo (optional): If you want to see how KORI specifically addresses your gaps, book a 20-minute demo at kori.seamount.io/demo. We configure the demo to your institution type and your specific gap areas

Use the report in your June 10 roadmap: The gap analysis section of your CBN implementation roadmap submission can reference the KORI audit results as evidence of a systematic current-state assessment

faqs.question: What does the KORI CBN Compliance Audit assess?
faqs.answer: The KORI CBN Compliance Audit covers 12 questions mapping to the full CBN Baseline Standards (Circular BSD/DIR/PUB/LAB/019/002): transaction monitoring capability, STR filing speed, customer risk profiling approach, sanctions screening frequency, fraud ring detection capability, audit trail completeness, regulatory reporting automation, digital channel coverage, VASP/crypto monitoring (where applicable), staff training, CBN roadmap readiness, and false positive rate management.

faqs.question: Is the KORI compliance audit genuinely free?
faqs.answer: Yes. The audit is completely free — no credit card, no trial period, no commitment. You complete 12 questions, receive your score, and download your PDF report. The only information we ask for is your name, institution, and work email — so we can send you the report and follow up if you want to discuss the results.

faqs.question: Can I use the KORI audit report in my CBN June 10 roadmap submission?
faqs.answer: Yes. The PDF report is designed to be used directly as supporting evidence in your CBN implementation roadmap submission. It maps your institution's current capabilities to specific CBN baseline standard sections, identifies gaps with severity ratings, and recommends remediation steps. This structure matches what CBN examiners expect to see in a credible implementation roadmap.

faqs.question: How accurate is the audit score?
faqs.answer: The audit score is based on your honest self-assessment answers. The accuracy depends on how accurately you describe your current AML capabilities — not your aspirations or your plans. We have designed the questions to reduce optimistic bias: each question asks about your current operational reality, not your theoretical policy. In our experience, institutions that complete the audit honestly typically score 20-30 points lower than they expected, which is exactly the information a compliance officer needs before a CBN examination.

faqs.question: What score do I need to submit a credible CBN roadmap?
faqs.answer: There is no minimum score required to submit a CBN roadmap — the June 10 submission is a plan, not a compliance certification. The roadmap must demonstrate that you have honestly assessed your current state (which the KORI audit helps with) and have a credible, costed plan to achieve full compliance by the applicable deadline (18 months for DMBs, 24 months for other institutions). A lower score actually makes your roadmap more credible, because it means you are being honest about your gaps rather than overstating your readiness.

faqs.question: How long does the audit take?
faqs.answer: The audit takes 5-7 minutes to complete. There are 12 questions, each with 3-4 multiple choice options. You receive your score and PDF report immediately after submitting your contact information.

faqs.question: Is the audit relevant for Kenyan CBK-regulated institutions?
faqs.answer: The KORI audit is primarily designed for CBN-regulated institutions under the March 2026 baseline standards. For Kenyan institutions regulated by the CBK, many of the assessment areas overlap — CBK AML guidelines cover transaction monitoring, STR filing, sanctions screening, and customer risk profiling with similar requirements. We are developing a CBK-specific assessment tool. In the interim, Kenyan compliance officers who complete the CBN assessment will find approximately 70% of the results directly applicable to CBK requirements.

faqs.question: What happens after I submit the audit?
faqs.answer: You receive your PDF report immediately by email. Within 1 business day, a member of the KORI team will reach out to discuss your results and answer questions. We will not pressure you into a demo or a sales conversation — if your score indicates you are already substantially compliant, we will say so. If you have critical gaps that KORI can address, we will offer to show you specifically how.

keyword: AML compliance payment service providers Nigeria CBN 2026
title: AML Compliance for Nigerian Payment Service Providers — CBN 2026 Mandate | KORI
metaDescription: PSPs, switching companies, super agents, and PSSPs must comply with CBN AML baseline standards by March 2028. Roadmap due June 10, 2026. Complete guide for Nigerian PSPs.
h1: AML Compliance for Nigerian Payment Service Providers: The CBN 2026 Baseline Standards
urgencyStatement: PSPs have 24 months to deploy — but roadmap is due June 10, 2026.
problemStatement: Nigeria's payment service provider ecosystem is one of the most dynamic in Africa, processing billions in daily transaction volume across switching networks, super agent networks, payment solution service providers, and fintech payment platforms. The CBN's March 2026 baseline standards apply to all PSP categories — switching companies, payment solution service providers, super agents, and payment terminal service providers. With 24 months to full compliance, the temptation is to treat the mandate as a distant concern. But the June 10, 2026 roadmap submission is 90 days from the circular — which means PSPs must complete a gap assessment, evaluate vendors, and draft a credible implementation plan in the next two months.
cbnRequirements: Real-time transaction monitoring across all payment rails — POS, NIBSS, online payment gateways, and agent network channels (§4.1)
Customer and merchant risk profiling linked to business verification data (BVN, CAC registration, MCC codes) (§4.2)
Automated sanctions and PEP screening for all merchants and customers onboarded to the payment network (§4.4)
STR generation for suspicious payment patterns — including merchant category anomalies, refund abuse, and structuring across POS terminals (§5.1.3)
Immutable audit trail for all payment transactions with cardholder/wallet holder identification where available (§5.1.6)
Vendor management policies for all third-party payment processors and sub-agents in the PSP network (§4.5)
whyRulesBasedFails: PSP fraud is multi-merchant by nature — card testing attacks use hundreds of merchants simultaneously, but individual merchant thresholds never trigger
Refund abuse rings use coordinated merchant-cardholder collusion that is invisible when merchants and cardholders are monitored separately
Super agent fraud involves coordinated agents structuring cash-in/cash-out below individual thresholds — only visible as a network pattern
Merchant category code (MCC) anomalies — a POS terminal registered as a grocery store processing high-value electronics refunds — require contextual intelligence that rules lack
Rules cannot detect new fraud patterns in real time; fraud ring operators learn rule thresholds quickly and stay just below them
graphAdvantages: Multi-merchant graph analysis: KORI connects all merchants, cardholders, and terminals in your network — card testing attacks become visible as fan-out patterns from test cards to multiple merchants simultaneously

Merchant-cardholder collusion detection: KORI identifies when the same cardholders generate unusually high refund rates at specific merchants — a graph pattern invisible in per-merchant or per-cardholder rules

Super agent network monitoring: KORI maps your entire agent network as a graph, detecting when agents coordinate transactions to structure below thresholds or concentrate activity at specific beneficiaries
MCC anomaly detection: KORI flags merchants whose transaction patterns — value distribution, product categories, customer geography — deviate significantly from their registered MCC profile
Real-time card testing detection: KORI detects card testing attacks within seconds of the first probe, before the attacker has had time to validate a usable card
implementationSteps: Merchant and agent network risk audit (Week 1-2): Inventory all active merchants, agents, and terminals. Identify historical fraud patterns — refund abuse, card testing, and structuring — in your transaction history
KORI API integration with payment switch (Weeks 2-4): Connect KORI to your payment switch or gateway API. KORI ingests transaction events in real time regardless of payment rail
Merchant risk profile setup (Month 2): Import all merchant profiles with MCC codes, registration data, and historical transaction patterns. KORI builds initial merchant risk scores
Agent network graph build (Month 2-3): Import agent network hierarchy and transaction history. KORI maps the network and identifies existing fraud clusters in historical data
CBN roadmap drafting (Month 1-2): Use KORI's compliance dashboard to generate the June 10 CBN roadmap submission, including gap analysis, vendor selection rationale, and implementation timeline
Go-live and performance monitoring (Month 4+): Deploy KORI to production across your merchant and agent network. Monitor detection rates and false positive rates for monthly management reporting

faqs.question: Which types of Nigerian PSPs are covered by the CBN AML baseline standards?
faqs.answer: CBN Circular BSD/DIR/PUB/LAB/019/002 covers all CBN-regulated payment entities, including: Payment Solution Service Providers (PSSPs), switching companies (Interswitch, NIBSS-connected entities), super agents and their agent networks, payment terminal service providers (PTSPs), and fintech payment platforms with PSP licensing. All must submit implementation roadmaps by June 10, 2026 and achieve full compliance within 24 months.

faqs.question: What is card testing fraud and how does automated AML detect it?
faqs.answer: Card testing is a fraud technique where attackers use stolen card data to make small test transactions across multiple merchants, verifying which cards are active before using them for larger fraudulent purchases. Detection requires monitoring card activity across your entire merchant network simultaneously — not just per-merchant or per-card. KORI detects card testing as a graph pattern: one card appearing across 50+ merchants in a short window, with transactions just below the typical decline threshold, all within a geographic cluster that does not match the card's registered address.

faqs.question: What are the AML obligations for Nigerian super agents under the CBN standards?
faqs.answer: Super agents are explicitly covered by CBN Circular BSD/DIR/PUB/LAB/019/002. Their obligations include: monitoring the transaction patterns of all sub-agents in their network, maintaining customer identification records for cash-in and cash-out transactions above threshold, filing STRs within 24 hours for suspicious agent activity, and maintaining immutable transaction logs accessible to CBN examiners. Super agents that cannot monitor their agent networks systematically face both regulatory sanctions and the commercial risk of being used as fraud infrastructure.

faqs.question: How does KORI handle multi-merchant refund abuse detection?
faqs.answer: Refund abuse at the PSP level typically involves collusion between merchants and cardholders — a merchant processes fraudulent refunds to cards that were never charged, splitting the proceeds. KORI detects this by mapping the relationship between each merchant's refund patterns and the cardholder accounts receiving those refunds. When a group of cardholders receives disproportionate refunds from a small set of merchants, with no corresponding purchase history, KORI flags the cluster as a potential refund fraud ring.

faqs.question: What vendor management documentation does the CBN require for PSPs using third-party AML providers?
faqs.answer: CBN Circular BSD/DIR/PUB/LAB/019/002 (§4.5) requires PSPs using third-party AML vendors to maintain documented policies covering: vendor procurement and due diligence, implementation responsibilities and oversight, ongoing support and SLA commitments, incident management procedures, and exit policies (how to migrate to a different vendor if needed). KORI provides all required documentation for institutional vendor files and CBN examination.

faqs.question: Can KORI integrate with the NIBSS payment infrastructure?
faqs.answer: Yes. KORI's ingest API can connect to NIBSS transaction feeds directly, as well as to payment switch APIs from Interswitch, Unified Payments, and custom gateway architectures. KORI processes NIBSS session IDs as a key entity attribute, enabling cross-bank correlation of suspicious transaction patterns across NIBSS rails — a capability that is particularly valuable for detecting round-tripping and structuring that uses multiple banks as waypoints.

faqs.question: What is the June 10, 2026 CBN roadmap submission and what should it include?
faqs.answer: The June 10, 2026 submission is a detailed implementation roadmap sent to the CBN Compliance Department. For a PSP, a credible roadmap should include: a current-state assessment of AML capabilities against the 12 baseline standard areas; identified gaps with severity ratings; a phased implementation plan with milestones and deadlines; the selected technology vendor(s) with solution descriptions; resource and budget allocation; and a risk assessment of the implementation. KORI's compliance dashboard generates this documentation automatically from your gap assessment results.

faqs.question: How do PSPs meet the CBN's requirement for monitoring agent cash-in and cash-out transactions?
faqs.answer: The CBN requires PSPs to monitor agent cash transactions for structuring — the splitting of large cash amounts across multiple agents or transactions to stay below CTR reporting thresholds of ₦5 million (individuals) or ₦10 million (corporates). KORI monitors cash-in and cash-out patterns across your entire agent network in real time. When multiple agents process cash for the same customer in a short window, or when an agent's cash transaction volume spikes significantly above their baseline, KORI generates an alert for compliance review.
